studies.title: Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (rTMS) for Adolescent Depression: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Treatment Response and Suicidal Ideation Reduction
studies.authors: Rakic L, Tas F, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: Major depressive disorder (MDD) affects 11-19% of adolescents globally; early treatment is vital given 46% relapse within 5 years. While rTMS is effective in adults, its use in adolescents is limited. Objectives: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluates rTMS efficacy and safety for adolescent MDD, with special attention to treatment response and suicidal ideation reduction. Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines, a comprehensive literature search was conducted using Embase, PubMed, PsycINFO, and the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews. Treatment response was defined as achieving a greater than 50% reduction in depressive complaints. Tolerability was systematically described. Suicidality was assessed as a specific secondary outcome. Results: [Details of quantitative results not accessible due to paywall; qualitative synthesis indicates evidence for rTMS efficacy in adolescent depression with acceptable tolerability and signals for suicidal ideation reduction.] Conclusions: This meta-analysis provides evidence regarding rTMS efficacy, tolerability, and effects on suicidal ideation in adolescents with MDD, with implications for expanding rTMS use in this population.

studies.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing the Efficacy of High-Frequency rTMS and Intermittent Theta-Burst Stimulation on Depressive and Anxiety Symptoms in Depressive Disorder
studies.authors: Opelka J, Albrecht J, Bezdíček O
studies.abstract: Background: The main objective was to compare the therapeutic efficacy of high-frequency rTMS (HF-rTMS) and intermittent theta-burst stimulation (iTBS) in reducing depressive and anxiety symptoms in patients with depressive disorder. Methods: This double-blind, randomized controlled trial was conducted at the psychiatric ward of Most Hospital (Czech Republic). Ninety-seven patients diagnosed with depressive disorder were randomly assigned to receive either HF-rTMS or iTBS, both targeting the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex. Data were collected using self-assessment and clinician-rated questionnaires (Zung Self-Rating Depression Scale, Beck Anxiety Inventory, Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale) before and after 10 stimulation sessions. Results: Both HF-rTMS and iTBS significantly reduced depressive and anxiety symptoms. No statistically significant differences were found between the two protocols in terms of clinical outcome. Conclusions: HF-rTMS and iTBS appear to have comparable efficacy for reducing depressive and anxiety symptoms in patients with depressive disorder.

studies.title: High-Definition Transcranial Direct Current Stimulation-Primed Intermittent Theta Burst Stimulation in Treatment of Negative Symptoms of Schizophrenia - A Randomised Sham Controlled Study
studies.abstract: Background: Negative symptoms in schizophrenia are a major unmet therapeutic need and may respond to neuromodulation. This was the first study assessing the effects of HD-tDCS primed iTBS in negative symptoms of schizophrenia. Methods: A prospective hospital-based randomized sham-controlled study was conducted at the Central Institute of Psychiatry, Ranchi, India. Forty participants were divided into active or sham HD-tDCS primed iTBS groups for a total of 20 sessions over two weeks. Results: Not available in abstract form. Conclusions: HD-tDCS primed iTBS is a novel combined neuromodulation approach with preliminary evidence of feasibility and potential benefit for negative symptoms in schizophrenia.

studies.title: Transdiagnostic Effectiveness of Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation for Mood and Anxiety Disorders
studies.abstract: Background and objectives: This study evaluated the transdiagnostic effectiveness of rTMS across mood and anxiety disorders in a real-world clinical setting. Methods: Ninety patients (46 women, 44 men; mean age 42.5 ± 16.9 years) were included. The majority (88.9%) had a primary diagnosis of major depressive disorder, 8.9% had bipolar depression, and two patients had primary anxiety disorders. Standard questionnaires assessed depression severity (QIDS; baseline mean 16.6 ± 4.9) and anxiety (GAD-7; baseline mean 12.7 ± 5.5). Patients received an average of 24.0 ± 7.0 treatments. Results: Average end-of-treatment scores for depression and anxiety severity were 10.5 ± 6.1 and 8.4 ± 5.8, respectively. Improvement and complete resolution rates for depressive symptoms were 64.4% and 28.7% respectively; for anxiety symptom improvement and complete resolution rates were 53.5% and 29.6%. Conclusions: rTMS shows transdiagnostic effectiveness for mood and anxiety disorders in real-world practice.

studies.title: Long-term outcomes following randomized rTMS or antidepressant treatment: A twelve-month naturalistic follow-up
studies.authors: Dalhuisen I, van Oostrom I, Spijker J, Wijnen B, van Exel E, van Mierlo H, de Waardt D, Arns M, Tendolkar I, van Eijndhoven P
studies.abstract: Background: This study investigated the long-term effects of rTMS and antidepressant medication in patients with moderate treatment-resistant depression (at least 2 treatment trials). Methods: Naturalistic follow-up data were collected for 12 months as part of a randomized clinical trial comparing rTMS (25 sessions targeting the left DLPFC; 10 Hz, 3000 pulses per session, 120% resting motor threshold) versus a switch of antidepressants according to the Dutch treatment algorithm, both combined with psychotherapy. Response and remission were assessed using the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HDRS-17) at end of treatment and at 4-, 6-, 9-, and 12-month follow-up. Results: At end of treatment, response and remission rates were higher in the rTMS group (40.9% and 29.5%) than in the medication group (18.8% and 6.3%). Response and remission remained stable over the full 12-month follow-up in the rTMS group. In the medication group, an increase in response and remission was observed at 4 months, after which these percentages also remained stable until the end of the study. Change in depression severity was larger after treatment with rTMS than with medication; the same was true for anxiety and anhedonia, whereas no significant difference between the two groups was seen for sleep, rumination, and cognitive reactivity. Loss-to-follow-up was substantial, with data available at 12 months for a little over half of the patients. Conclusions: These findings describe the clinical course of patients after acute rTMS or antidepressant treatment in treatment-resistant depression and highlight the need for future studies evaluating maintenance strategies.
